key_insight: A two-step seismic workflow combining matched field processing and source relocation can map glacier-surface crevasse opening with meter-scale precision and estimate propagation rates.
engineering_relevance: The method improves cryoseismic source localization under sparse instrumentation, enabling better assessment of fracture evolution, meltwater routing, and brittle-to-viscous transition behavior in glaciers.
method: Use matched field processing to detect surface icequakes and separate them from meltwater noise.
Relocate discrete arrivals to refine source positions and reveal crevasse opening episodes.
Estimate crevasse propagation rate and diffusion coefficients from the localized events.
evidence_signal: Applied to Hansbreen, Svalbard, the workflow identified surface icequakes and yielded diffusion coefficients of 0.47 to 0.55 m2/s from the relocated events.
watch_item: Performance and localization accuracy may degrade in sparse or irregular seismic arrays, so the approach remains sensitive to network geometry and arrival-time quality.
paper_title: Surface Crevasse Evolution Observed Using Matched Field Processing and Source Relocation at Hansbreen, Svalbard
